sanskrit:
धृतराष्ट्र उवाच।
धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः।
मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय॥
iast:
dhṛtarāṣṭra uvāca
dharma-kṣetre kuru-kṣetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ
māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāś caiva kim akurvata sañjaya
translation: Dhritarashtra said: On the field of dharma, the field of the Kurus, gathered eager for battle, what did my sons and the Pandavas do, O Sanjaya?

sanskrit:
कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन।
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि॥
iast:
karmaṇy-evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana
mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi
translation: You have the right to action alone, never to its fruits. Let not the fruits of action be your motive, nor let your attachment be to inaction.

commentary: Action is the field of human authority; results are governed by larger forces (svabhava, time, divine ordinance, the field of karma). The verse therefore prescribes acting fully and skilfully while renouncing the demand that the action *cause* a specific outcome. The Advaita reading further interprets this as a movement toward niṣkāma-karma — work performed without ego-claim — which clears the mind for jñāna-yoga. The four prohibitions are: (1) do not claim the fruit, (2) do not act with the fruit as motive, (3) do not become the cause of fruit-attachment, (4) do not collapse into inaction.

Vishishtadvaita reads the verse as a teaching of karma-yoga performed as service (kainkarya) to the Lord, who alone is the dispenser of fruits. One acts not for one's own enjoyment but as offering; the fruits belong to Bhagavan. The renunciation here is not of action but of self-arrogating attachment to results, since attributing results to oneself usurps what is properly the Lord's prerogative.

sanskrit:
योगस्थः कुरु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा धनञ्जय।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः समो भूत्वा समत्वं योग उच्यते॥
iast:
yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā dhanañjaya
siddhy-asiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvaṁ yoga ucyate
translation: Established in yoga, perform actions abandoning attachment, O Dhananjaya. Equanimous in success and failure — this evenness of mind is called yoga.

commentary: The verse offers the Gita's working definition of yoga: samatvam, evenness or equipoise of mind across the polarities of success and failure. Practice consists of acting from a centre that does not oscillate with outcome. This equipoise is both the prerequisite for skilled action and its fruit.

sanskrit:
यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत।
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम्॥
iast:
yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata
abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṁ sṛjāmy aham
translation: Whenever, O Bharata, righteousness declines and unrighteousness rises, then I manifest Myself.

sanskrit:
परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम्।
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे॥
iast:
paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṁ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām
dharma-saṁsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge
translation: For the protection of the virtuous, the destruction of the wicked, and the establishment of dharma, I come into being in age after age.

sanskrit:
अनन्याश्चिन्तयन्तो मां ये जनाः पर्युपासते।
तेषां नित्याभियुक्तानां योगक्षेमं वहाम्यहम्॥
iast:
ananyāś cintayanto māṁ ye janāḥ paryupāsate
teṣāṁ nityābhiyuktānāṁ yoga-kṣemaṁ vahāmy aham
translation: To those who worship Me with undivided focus, ever steadfast — to them I bring fulfillment of needs (yoga) and protection of what they have (kshema).

sanskrit:
सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज।
अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः॥
iast:
sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṁ śaraṇaṁ vraja
ahaṁ tvāṁ sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ
translation: Abandoning all duties, take refuge in Me alone. I shall liberate you from all sins. Do not grieve.

commentary: Known as the charama-shloka — the final/concluding verse of the Gita's teaching. Vishishtadvaita reads this as the central injunction of prapatti (total surrender): all dharmas as means are renounced in favour of the Lord Himself as the sole means and end. The renunciation is not of dharma's fruits but of dharma as a self-sufficient path; the soul takes Bhagavan as both refuge and refuge-giver.

Advaita reads sarva-dharman parityajya as the renunciation of all action-categories (action and abstention alike) when the seeker has matured into knowledge of the non-dual Self (atman = brahman). The 'taking refuge in Me alone' is then the abidance in the unconditioned Self that is one's own true nature; liberation from sins follows because sins, being products of avidya, dissolve when their root-ignorance is uprooted.
